full_response:
We recognize Ben’s data structure as a Bloom filter with no delete operation.  
Let us reason carefully about the two types of errors.

1.  False negative (“Miss”):  
    Definition LOOKUP(w) returns FALSE even though w has previously been inserted.

    Observation INSERT(w) sets every bit in the k‐tuple  
    A[h1(w)], … , A[hk(w)] to 1.  
    After that moment those bits are never reset to 0 (the structure contains no
    delete or re-initialization step).  
    Therefore, whenever the same word w is queried later, the
    identical k positions are inspected and each of them is still 1.  
    Hence LOOKUP(w) must return TRUE.

    Conclusion A false negative cannot occur.

2.  False positive (“False hit”):  
    Definition LOOKUP(w′) returns TRUE even though w′ has never been inserted.

    Because many different words share the same table,
    it is possible that during the insertion of other words
    all k positions h1(w′), … , hk(w′) have been set to 1 “by accident”.
    When w′ is queried, the test sees k ones and therefore returns TRUE
    although w′ itself was never inserted.

    This event happens with strictly positive probability
    (and is in fact the whole point of the Bloom-filter error analysis),
    so false positives are possible.

\boxed{\text{False negatives: No \qquad False positives: Yes}}

Confidence: 0.77
